title_en: The First Principle: Knowledge of Your Lord (Ma'rifat Allah)
content_id:
Prinsip pertama dan yang paling fundamental adalah pengetahuan tentang Allah — mengetahui siapa Tuhanmu, apa yang diperintahkan-Nya, dan apa artinya menyembah-Nya saja. Ibn Abdul Wahhab menyajikan prinsip ini dengan pertanyaan langsung: 'Siapakah Tuhanmu?' Jawabannya adalah: 'Tuhanku adalah Allah, yang memeliharaku dan seluruh ciptaan dengan nikmat-nikmat-Nya. Hanya Dia tuhanku dan aku tidak memiliki tuhan selain-Nya.' Dalilnya adalah ayat: 'Segala puji bagi Allah, Tuhan semesta alam' (Al-Fatihah, 1:2).

Pengarang menjelaskan bahwa 'Tuhan' (Rabb) dalam Islam memiliki makna yang komprehensif. Kata itu merujuk kepada Pencipta yang memiliki, memelihara, dan mengatur semua yang ada. Segalanya di langit dan di bumi berada di bawah kekuasaan-Nya. Dia memberi kehidupan dan menyebabkan kematian, menyediakan rezeki, mengendalikan semua urusan, dan tidak ada yang terjadi kecuali dengan kehendak dan pengetahuan-Nya. Mengakui hal ini adalah fondasi tauhid ar-rububiyyah — keesaan Allah dalam ketuhanan-Nya.

Namun, Ibn Abdul Wahhab memperjelas bahwa sekadar pengakuan atas ketuhanan Allah tidak cukup untuk keselamatan. Bahkan kaum musyrikin Makkah yang diperangi Nabi pun mengakui bahwa Allah adalah Pencipta dan Pemelihara. Allah berfirman: 'Dan jika kamu bertanya kepada mereka siapa yang menciptakan langit dan bumi dan menundukkan matahari dan bulan, pasti mereka akan menjawab, 'Allah'' (Al-Ankabut, 29:61). Yang gagal mereka lakukan adalah mengarahkan ibadah mereka secara eksklusif kepada-Nya — dan di sinilah mereka jatuh ke dalam syirik.

Ini mengarah pada dimensi kedua mengenal Allah: tauhid al-uluhiyyah, atau keesaan Allah dalam ibadah. Kata 'ilah' (tuhan) berarti satu yang disembah dengan cinta, rasa takut, harapan, dan penghormatan. Ibn Abdul Wahhab mendefinisikan ibadah ('ibadah) sebagai istilah yang komprehensif untuk segala sesuatu yang Allah cintai dan ridhai, baik tindakan lahiriah maupun keadaan batiniah. Ini mencakup shalat, puasa, doa, tawakal, cinta, rasa takut, dan harapan. Semua ini harus diarahkan kepada Allah saja, tanpa sekutu atau perantara.

Pengarang selanjutnya menyajikan tiga kategori tauhid yang telah disimpulkan oleh ulama-ulama Ahl al-Sunnah dari Alquran dan Sunnah: tauhid ar-rububiyyah (keesaan dalam ketuhanan), tauhid al-uluhiyyah (keesaan dalam ibadah), dan tauhid al-asma was-sifat (keesaan dalam nama-nama dan sifat-sifat). Dalam kategori terakhir ini, kaum Muslim menegaskan nama-nama dan sifat-sifat yang Allah tegaskan untuk diri-Nya dalam Alquran dan yang Nabi tegaskan dalam Sunnah — tanpa distorsi, pengingkaran, penyerupaan dengan ciptaan, atau bertanya 'bagaimana caranya.'

Ibn Abdul Wahhab menutup bagian ini dengan menekankan bukti ibadah yang ditemukan dalam alam itu sendiri. Beliau mengutip firman Allah: 'Sesungguhnya dalam penciptaan langit dan bumi, dan pergantian malam dan siang terdapat tanda-tanda bagi orang yang berakal' (Ali Imran, 3:190). Alam semesta bersaksi tentang Penciptanya. Muslim yang merenungkan ciptaan dengan hati yang sehat tidak bisa tidak sampai pada pengakuan, rasa syukur, dan penyerahan diri sepenuhnya kepada Allah — yang merupakan makna Islam itu sendiri.
content_en:
The first and most fundamental of the three principles is knowledge of Allah — knowing who your Lord is, what He has commanded, and what it means to worship Him alone. Ibn Abdul Wahhab presents this principle with a direct question: "Who is your Lord?" The answer is: "My Lord is Allah, who raised me and all of creation with His blessings. He alone is my deity and I have no deity other than Him." The proof is the verse: "Praise be to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (Al-Fatiha, 1:2).

The author explains that "Lord" (Rabb) in Islam carries a comprehensive meaning. It refers to the Creator who owns, sustains, and governs all that exists. Everything in the heavens and earth is under His dominion. He gives life and causes death, provides sustenance, controls all affairs, and nothing occurs except by His will and knowledge. Recognizing this is the foundation of tawhid ar-rububiyyah — the oneness of Allah in His lordship.

However, Ibn Abdul Wahhab makes clear that mere acknowledgment of Allah's lordship is insufficient for salvation. Even the polytheists of Makkah whom the Prophet fought acknowledged that Allah was the Creator and Sustainer. Allah says: "And if you asked them who created the heavens and the earth and subjected the sun and the moon, they would surely say, 'Allah'" (Al-Ankabut, 29:61). What they failed to do was direct their worship exclusively to Him — and this is where they fell into shirk.

This leads to the second dimension of knowing Allah: tawhid al-uluhiyyah, or the oneness of Allah in worship. The word "ilah" (deity) means the one who is worshipped with love, fear, hope, and reverence. Ibn Abdul Wahhab defines worship ('ibadah) as a comprehensive term for everything that Allah loves and is pleased with, whether outward actions or inward states. This includes prayer, fasting, supplication, reliance, love, fear, and hope. All of these must be directed to Allah alone, with no partner or intermediary.

The author further presents the three categories of tawhid that scholars of Ahl al-Sunnah have derived from the Quran and Sunnah: tawhid ar-rububiyyah (oneness in lordship), tawhid al-uluhiyyah (oneness in worship), and tawhid al-asma' was-sifat (oneness in names and attributes). In this last category, Muslims affirm the names and attributes that Allah has affirmed for Himself in the Quran and that the Prophet affirmed in the Sunnah — without distortion, denial, resemblance to creation, or asking "how."

Ibn Abdul Wahhab concludes this section by emphasizing the proof of worship found in nature itself. He cites Allah's words: "Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of the night and the day are signs for those of understanding" (Al 'Imran, 3:190). The universe testifies to its Creator. The Muslim who reflects on creation with a sound heart cannot but arrive at recognition of, gratitude toward, and complete submission to Allah — which is the very meaning of Islam.
content_ar: The أولاً and most الأساسي of the three principles is العلم of الله — knowing who your Lord is, what He has commanded, and what it means للعبادة Him alone. Ibn Abdul Wahhab presents this principle with a direct question: "Who is your Lord?" The answer is: "My Lord is الله, who raised me and all of creation with His البركات. He alone is my deity and I have no deity other than Him." The proof is the الآية: "Praise be to الله, the Lord of all the worlds" (Al-Fatiha, 1:2). The author explains that "Lord" (Rabb) in الإسلام carries a comprehensive meaning. It refers to the Creator who owns, sustains, and governs all that exists. Everything in the heavens and earth is under His dominion. He gives الحياة and causes الموت, provides sustenance, controls all affairs, and nothing occurs except by His will and العلم. Recognizing this is the foundation of tawhid ar-rububiyyah — the oneness of الله in His lordship. غير أن, Ibn Abdul Wahhab makes clear that mere acknowledgment of الله's lordship is insufficient for النجاة. Even the polytheists of مكة المكرمة whom النبي ﷺ fought acknowledged that الله was the Creator and Sustainer. الله says: "And إذا you asked them who created the heavens and the earth and subjected the sun and the moon, they would surely say, 'الله'" (Al-Ankabut, 29:61). What they failed to do was direct their worship exclusively to Him — and this is where they fell into الشرك. This leads to the ثانياً dimension of knowing الله: tawhid al-uluhiyyah, or the oneness of الله in worship. The word "ilah" (deity) means the one who is worshipped with المحبة, الخوف, الأمل, and reverence. Ibn Abdul Wahhab defines worship ('ibadah) as a comprehensive term for everything that الله loves and is pleased with, whether outward actions or inward states. This includes الصلاة, الصيام, الدعاء, reliance, المحبة, الخوف, and الأمل. All of these must be directed to الله alone, with no partner or intermediary. The author further presents the three categories of tawhid that العلماء of Ahl al-السنة have derived from the القرآن الكريم and السنة: tawhid ar-rububiyyah (oneness in lordship), tawhid al-uluhiyyah (oneness in worship), and tawhid al-asma' was-sifat (oneness in names and attributes). In this last category, المسلمون affirm the names and attributes that الله has affirmed for Himself in the القرآن الكريم and that النبي ﷺ affirmed in the السنة — without distortion, denial, resemblance to creation, or asking "how." Ibn Abdul Wahhab concludes this الفصل by emphasizing the proof of worship found in nature itself. He cites الله's words: "Indeed, in the creation of the heavens and the earth and the alternation of the night and the day are signs for those of الفهم" (Al 'Imran, 3:190). The universe testifies to its Creator. The مسلم who reflects on creation with a sound القلب cannot but arrive at recognition of, الشكر toward, and complete submission to الله — which is the very meaning of الإسلام.
